front: Palantir Technologies was founded in {{c1::2003}} in {{c2::Palo Alto, CA}}.
back: Founded 2003, Palo Alto, California.

front: The five co-founders of Palantir are {{c1::Peter Thiel}}, {{c2::Alex Karp}}, {{c3::Joe Lonsdale}}, {{c4::Stephen Cohen}}, and {{c5::Nathan Gettings}}.
back: Peter Thiel (Chairman), Alex Karp (CEO), Joe Lonsdale, Stephen Cohen, Nathan Gettings.

front: Peter Thiel co-founded {{c1::PayPal}}, runs {{c2::Founders Fund}}, and was an early investor in {{c3::Facebook}}.
back: PayPal co-founder, Founders Fund, early Facebook investor.

front: Alex Karp holds a PhD in {{c1::social theory}} from {{c2::Goethe University Frankfurt}}, where he studied under {{c3::Jürgen Habermas}}.
back: PhD in social theory, Goethe University Frankfurt, studied under Jürgen Habermas.

front: Alex Karp became Palantir's CEO in {{c1::2005}}.
Joe Lonsdale later founded {{c1::Formation 8}}, {{c2::8VC}}, and {{c3::Addepar}}.
back: Formation 8, 8VC, Addepar.

front: {{c1::Stephen Cohen}} built Palantir's first working prototype as a Stanford CS undergrad.
back: Stephen Cohen — Stanford CS, first prototype builder.

front: Peter Thiel personally invested approximately ${{c1::30 million}} in Palantir's early years.
back: $30 million from Thiel's personal funds.

front: The company name 'Palantir' comes from {{c1::J.R.R. Tolkien}}'s *The Lord of the Rings*. The palantíri were {{c2::crystal 'seeing stones'}} that let users see events across vast distances.
back: Tolkien's Lord of the Rings — the palantíri were seeing stones for intelligence gathering.

front: Palantir's founding philosophy: the technology enables {{c1::sight}}, but the {{c2::human}} must interpret wisely. Software {{c3::augments}} human intelligence; it does not {{c4::replace}} judgment.
back: Technology enables sight, humans interpret. Augment, not replace.

front: Palantir's first and most important early backer was {{c1::In-Q-Tel}}, the venture capital arm of the {{c2::CIA}}, which invested approximately ${{c3::2 million}}.
back: In-Q-Tel (CIA's VC arm) invested ~$2 million.

front: In-Q-Tel was established in {{c1::1999}} as an independent, nonprofit VC firm to identify cutting-edge commercial technologies for {{c2::U.S. national security}}.
back: Founded 1999, identifies commercial tech for national security.

front: The tight feedback loop of engineers and end-users iterating together in the field became the foundation of Palantir's operating model called {{c1::forward deployment}}.
back: Forward deployment — engineers embedded with users at client sites.

front: Palantir launched {{c1::Foundry}} in {{c2::2013}}, pivoting from a government-only company to a {{c3::dual-track}} (government + commercial) strategy.
back: Foundry launched 2013 — enabled the commercial business.

front: In {{c1::2019}}, Palantir relocated its headquarters from Palo Alto to {{c2::Denver, Colorado}}.
back: HQ moved to Denver, CO in 2019.

front: Palantir went public via a {{c1::direct listing}} on the {{c2::NYSE}} on {{c3::September 30, 2020}} with ticker {{c4::PLTR}}. Reference price was ${{c5::7.25}}/share.
back: Direct listing, NYSE, Sept 30, 2020, ticker PLTR, $7.25 reference price.

front: Palantir's FY2020 (IPO year) revenue was approximately ${{c1::1.1 billion}}.
back: ~$1.1B FY2020 revenue.

front: {{c1::AIP (Artificial Intelligence Platform)}} was launched in {{c2::May 2023}} and connected LLMs to enterprise data through the Ontology.
back: AIP launched May 2023 — LLMs connected to the Ontology.

front: In 2024, Palantir's stock was the {{c1::#1 performer in the S&P 500}}, up approximately {{c2::340%}} for the year.
back: #1 S&P 500 performer, up ~340% in 2024.

front: Palantir's FY2025 total revenue was approximately ${{c1::4.48 billion}} (+{{c2::56%}} YoY) with approximately {{c3::3,936}} employees.
back: $4.48B revenue, +56% YoY, ~3,936 employees.

front: Palantir's FY2025 revenue per employee is approximately ${{c1::1.14 million}}.
back: ~$1.14M revenue per employee.

front: FY2025 U.S. Government revenue was ~${{c1::1.855B}} (+{{c2::55%}} YoY).
FY2025 U.S. Commercial revenue was ~${{c1::1.465B}} (+{{c2::109%}} YoY), driven primarily by {{c3::AIP}} adoption.
back: $1.465B, +109% YoY, driven by AIP.

front: In August 2025, the U.S. Army awarded Palantir a {{c1::10-year}} Enterprise Service Agreement (ESA) worth up to ${{c2::10 billion}}, consolidating {{c3::75}} existing software contracts.
back: 10-year, up to $10B Army ESA, consolidating 75 contracts.

front: Palantir won the {{c1::Maven Smart System}} contract — total Maven-related Army awards exceed ${{c2::1 billion}}.
back: Maven Smart System, >$1B combined.

front: The UK Ministry of Defence awarded Palantir a three-year, {{c1::£240.6 million}} contract in December 2025.
back: £240.6M, 3-year UK MoD contract (Dec 2025).

front: NHS England awarded Palantir a seven-year, {{c1::£330 million}} contract for a {{c2::Federated Data Platform (FDP)}} in November 2023.
back: £330M, 7-year, Federated Data Platform for NHS.

front: The CDC awarded Palantir a five-year, ${{c1::443 million}} contract in December 2022 to create a '{{c2::Common Operating Picture}}' for public health.
back: $443M, 5-year CDC contract, Common Operating Picture.

front: At Palantir, a Forward Deployed Engineer (FDE) is internally called '{{c1::Delta}}' and a Deployment Strategist is internally called '{{c2::Echo}}'.
back: FDE = Delta, DS = Echo.

front: Palantir's culture is organized around small, autonomous teams of {{c1::3-8 people}} called {{c2::micro-teams}}.
back: 3-8 person micro-teams.

front: Palantir's three core values are: {{c1::Innovation}}, {{c2::Mission-Driven Impact}}, and {{c3::Meritocracy}}.
back: Innovation, Mission-Driven Impact, Meritocracy.

front: The {{c1::Meritocracy Fellowship}} (launched {{c2::2024}}) offers {{c3::high school graduates}} full-time work at Palantir as an alternative to a four-year degree.
back: Meritocracy Fellowship, 2024, high-school-grad alternative to college.

front: Palantir's ICE tools include {{c1::ICM (Investigative Case Management)}}, {{c2::FALCON}}, and {{c3::ImmigrationOS}}.
back: ICM, FALCON, ImmigrationOS — tools for ICE/HSI.

front: {{c1::Google}} originally led Project Maven but withdrew in 2019 under employee protest. {{c2::Palantir}} stepped in and built the Maven Smart System.
back: Google withdrew from Project Maven → Palantir took over.

front: Palantir's gross margin is approximately {{c1::80%+}}.
back: ~80%+ gross margin.

front: Palantir's market capitalization surpassed ${{c1::430 billion}} by late 2025, placing it among the top {{c2::30}} most valuable U.S. companies.
back: $430B+ market cap, top 30 U.S. companies.

front: In February 2025, Karp and co-author {{c1::Nicholas Zamiska}} published '{{c2::The Technological Republic: Hard Power, Soft Belief, and the Future of the West}}'.
back: Nicholas Zamiska co-authored 'The Technological Republic' with Karp, Feb 2025.

front: Karp describes himself as a '{{c1::progressive}}' and '{{c2::socialist}}' who is simultaneously a '{{c3::techno-nationalist}}' and defense hawk.
back: Progressive socialist + techno-nationalist defense hawk.

front: Palantir's defense prime competitors include {{c1::Raytheon/RTX}}, {{c2::Lockheed Martin}}, {{c3::Northrop Grumman}}, and {{c4::L3Harris}}.
back: Raytheon/RTX, Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, L3Harris.

front: Palantir's consulting firm competitors include {{c1::Accenture Federal Services}}, {{c2::Deloitte}}, and {{c3::Booz Allen Hamilton}}.
back: Accenture Federal Services, Deloitte, Booz Allen Hamilton.

front: Palantir's data platform competitors include {{c1::Snowflake}} and {{c2::Databricks}}. In 2025, Palantir and Databricks formalized a {{c3::strategic partnership}}.
back: Snowflake, Databricks. Databricks partnership in 2025.

front: Palantir's PayPal fraud origin: fraud accounted for over {{c1::1%}} of PayPal's total transaction volume — an existential threat.
back: >1% of transaction volume was fraud.

front: '{{c1::AIPCon}}' is Palantir's marquee customer conference showcasing real-world AIP deployments.
back: AIPCon — Palantir's customer conference.

front: Karp called Google's protesting employees '{{c1::nihilists}}' and argued military AI is essential for {{c2::U.S. national security}}.
back: Called Google protesters 'nihilists'; military AI essential for national security.

front: Palantir's founding thesis on the privacy-security balance: '{{c1::privacy and security are not in conflict}}'— the right technology enables more {{c2::targeted}}, {{c3::auditable}}, and {{c4::civil-liberties-respecting}} intelligence analysis.
back: Privacy and security are not in conflict — technology can be targeted, auditable, and civil-liberties-respecting.

front: Karp described Palantir's culture as an '{{c1::artist colony}}' — a deliberate contrast to command-and-control defense contractors.
back: 'Artist colony' — not a traditional corporate hierarchy.

front: In late 2025, Karp described Palantir's culture as intentionally '{{c1::tribal and cultish}}'.
back: 'Tribal and cultish' — selectivity as a feature.

front: Palantir's four core platforms are {{c1::Gotham}}, {{c2::Foundry}}, {{c3::Apollo}}, and {{c4::AIP}}.
back: Gotham, Foundry, Apollo, AIP.

front: Palantir's Three Pillars: Foundry/Gotham = '{{c1::The What}}', The Ontology = '{{c2::The How}}', Apollo = '{{c3::The Where}}'.
back: The What (platforms), The How (ontology/meaning), The Where (deployment).

front: {{c1::Gotham}} is Palantir's platform for {{c2::defense and intelligence}}, first developed in {{c3::2008}}.
back: Gotham — defense & intelligence, 2008.

front: Gotham's defining capability is {{c1::multi-INT fusion}} — integrating {{c2::SIGINT}}, {{c3::HUMINT}}, {{c4::GEOINT}}, {{c5::OSINT}}, and {{c6::MASINT}} into one platform.
back: Multi-INT fusion: SIGINT, HUMINT, GEOINT, OSINT, MASINT.

front: SIGINT stands for {{c1::Signals Intelligence}} — intercepted {{c2::communications and electronic signals}}.
back: Signals Intelligence — phone metadata, radio intercepts, cyber traffic.

front: HUMINT stands for {{c1::Human Intelligence}} — information from {{c2::human sources}}.
back: Human Intelligence — informant reports, debriefings.

front: GEOINT stands for {{c1::Geospatial Intelligence}} — {{c2::imagery and geospatial data}} like satellite photos and drone footage.
back: Geospatial Intelligence — satellite imagery, terrain maps.

front: OSINT stands for {{c1::Open-Source Intelligence}} — {{c2::publicly available information}} like news and social media.
back: Open-Source Intelligence — news, social media, public records.

front: MASINT stands for {{c1::Measurement & Signature Intelligence}} — {{c2::technical measurements of physical phenomena}} like radar signatures.
back: Measurement & Signature Intelligence — radar, nuclear radiation, chemical traces.

front: Gotham's five architecture layers (bottom to top): {{c1::Federation}} → {{c2::Entity Resolution}} → {{c3::Ontology}} → {{c4::Query}} → {{c5::Visualization}}.
back: Federation → Entity Resolution → Ontology → Query → Visualization.

front: {{c1::Gaia}} is Gotham's collaborative {{c2::geospatial command and control}} application for map-based analysis.
back: Gaia — Gotham's geospatial/C2 map application.

front: Gotham's Entity Resolution layer uses {{c1::fuzzy matching}}, {{c2::feature generation}}, {{c3::ML models}}, and {{c4::human-in-the-loop validation}} to de-duplicate records across sources.
back: Fuzzy matching, feature generation, ML, human-in-the-loop validation.

front: {{c1::Pattern-of-Life}} detection analyzes temporal and spatial data over time to identify habitual behaviors — movement patterns, communication schedules, meeting locations.
back: Pattern-of-Life — habitual behavioral analysis over time.

front: {{c1::COVs}} (Common Operating Views) are customizable dashboards in Gotham for any entity or operation.
back: COVs — Common Operating Views in Gotham.

front: {{c1::Foundry}} is Palantir's platform for {{c2::commercial enterprises and civil government agencies}}, often described as an '{{c3::enterprise operating system}}'.
back: Foundry — enterprise OS for commercial + civil government.

front: Explain the difference between Gotham and Foundry: who uses each, what each does, and how they relate.
back: **Gotham** = defense & intelligence (CIA, NSA, SOCOM) — fuses multi-INT data into actionable intelligence for military/intel operations. **Foundry** = commercial enterprises & civil government (hospitals, manufacturers, FDA) — integrates enterprise data into an 'operating system' for operational decisions. **Relationship**: Both build on the same Ontology architecture (objects, links, actions). Gotham models intelligence entities; Foundry models business entities. AIP sits on top of both, Apollo deploys both.

front: Foundry's four architecture layers (bottom to top): {{c1::Connectors}} → {{c2::Transformation Engine}} → {{c3::Ontology}} → {{c4::Application Layer}}.
back: Connectors → Transformation Engine → Ontology → Application Layer.

front: Foundry's three data transformation tools: {{c1::Pipeline Builder}} (point-and-click), {{c2::Code Workbook}} (interactive Python/SQL/R), and {{c3::Code Repository}} (production-grade, Git-based).
back: Pipeline Builder, Code Workbook, Code Repository.

front: Explain the progression: Pipeline Builder → Code Workbook → Code Repository. Who uses each and when?
back:
**Pipeline Builder** = citizen developers / ops analysts — point-and-click pipeline building, no code. Best for standard data integrations.
**Code Workbook** = data analysts prototyping — interactive Python/SQL/R coding environment for exploratory analysis and quick transforms.
**Code Repository** = production engineers — full Git-based IDE with CI/CD, unit testing, and peer review. Highest governance. DS helps customers choose the right tool for each team.

front: Foundry's four application-building tools are {{c1::Workshop}} (low-code builder), {{c2::Slate}} (flexible HTML/CSS/JS builder), {{c3::Object Explorer}} (search & discovery), and {{c4::Quiver}} (BI & analytics).
back: Workshop, Slate, Object Explorer, Quiver.

front: {{c1::Workshop}} is Palantir's flagship application builder for operational apps. It uses pre-built UI widgets that natively understand the {{c2::Ontology}}.
back: Workshop — flagship no-code app builder on Ontology widgets.

front: {{c1::Slate}} offers maximum flexibility with custom {{c2::HTML, CSS, and JavaScript}} but has {{c3::higher maintenance burden}} than Workshop.
back: Slate — custom HTML/CSS/JS, flexible but harder to maintain.

front: {{c1::Apollo}} is Palantir's {{c2::continuous delivery and infrastructure management}} platform — tagline: '{{c3::Mission Control for Software Deployment}}'.
back: Apollo — continuous delivery, 'Mission Control for Software Deployment.'

front: Apollo operates on a {{c1::hub-and-spoke}} architecture: a central {{c2::SaaS hub}} orchestrates distributed {{c3::spoke environments}} (Kubernetes clusters).
back: Hub-and-spoke: SaaS hub orchestrates spoke Kubernetes clusters.

front: For air-gapped networks, Apollo deploys {{c1::Remote Hubs}} within isolated networks. The {{c2::Binary Transfer Service (BTS)}} automates secure software transfer across security domains.
back: Remote Hubs inside air-gapped networks; BTS for secure cross-domain transfer.

front: Apollo holds U.S. government accreditations at DoD Impact Level {{c1::5}} (IL5) and Impact Level {{c2::6}} (IL6).
back: IL5 and IL6 accreditations.

front: Apollo can deploy to extreme edge environments including {{c1::submarines}}, {{c2::forward operating bases}}, {{c3::drones}}, {{c4::Humvees}}, and {{c5::disconnected aircraft}}.
back: Submarines, FOBs, drones, Humvees, aircraft.

front: Explain why Apollo is often called Palantir's 'deepest moat'. What strategic advantage does it provide?
back: 1. **Lock-in**: Once Apollo manages your infra, switching costs are enormous. 2. **Enables gov contracts**: DoD requires classified-network deployment — Apollo was built for IL5/IL6/air-gapped from day one, competitors can't match. 3. **Third-party deployment**: Apollo can deploy *any* containerized software, not just Palantir's. 4. **Speed**: While competitors spend months on FedRAMP certification, Apollo already has the accreditations. No other commercial software company can deploy from cloud to submarine to fighter jet.

front: {{c1::AIP}} (Artificial Intelligence Platform) was launched in {{c2::April 2023}} and is Palantir's {{c3::generative AI layer}} connecting LLMs to enterprise data through the Ontology.
back: AIP — generative AI layer, April 2023, LLMs + Ontology.

front: Explain how Palantir AIP differs from 'just using ChatGPT'. Give at least 4 dimensions.
back:
1. **Data Access**: ChatGPT has public internet data. AIP is grounded in the org's private Ontology — real, live data.
2. **Hallucination**: ChatGPT free-generates. AIP constrains LLMs to query verified Ontology objects (ontology-grounded AI).
3. **Action**: ChatGPT generates text. AIP triggers real operational actions (create orders, reassign resources).
4. **Security**: ChatGPT = single API key. AIP = purpose-based access control, LLM sees only what's essential.
5. **Audit**: ChatGPT = minimal logging. AIP = every LLM interaction logged and traceable.
6. **Deployment**: ChatGPT = cloud-only. AIP = cloud, on-prem, edge, air-gapped via Apollo.

front: AIP's three key components are {{c1::AIP Logic}} (build LLM functions), {{c2::Agent Studio}} (deploy AI agents), and {{c3::AIP Evals}} (test AI performance).
back: Logic (build), Agent Studio (deploy), Evals (test).

front: {{c1::AIP Logic}} is a {{c2::no-code}} development environment for building, testing, and deploying LLM-powered functions chained with Ontology data.
back: AIP Logic — no-code LLM function builder.

front: {{c1::Agent Studio}} creates interactive AI assistants (AIP Agents) powered by LLMs + Ontology + documents + custom tools.
back: Agent Studio — create/deploy AIP Agents.

front: {{c1::AIP Evals}} tests AI performance using test cases, '{{c2::LLM-as-a-judge}}' patterns, and human feedback integration.
back: AIP Evals — testing with LLM-as-a-judge and human feedback.

front: AIP prevents hallucination through five methods: {{c1::Ontology grounding}}, {{c2::Tool Use pattern}}, {{c3::metadata augmentation}}, {{c4::computational delegation}}, and {{c5::evaluation loops}}.
back: Ontology grounding, Tool Use, metadata augmentation, computational delegation, evaluation loops.

front: The key phrase for AIP in interviews is '{{c1::ontology-grounded AI}}' — LLMs constrained to query verified Ontology objects.
back: 'Ontology-grounded AI.'

front: {{c1::AIP Bootcamps}} are intensive {{c2::five-day}} workshops where customers bring their own data and build working AI applications. Over {{c3::1,300}} bootcamps were completed by mid-2024.
back: 5-day workshops, 1,300+ completed by mid-2024.

front: Palantir's Net Dollar Retention in 2025 was {{c1::134%}}, meaning existing customers expanded spending by 34%.
back: 134% net dollar retention.

front: AIP and Foundry together comprise over {{c1::300}} microservices and assets within a zero-trust security infrastructure.
back: 300+ microservices.

front: AIPCon notable customers: {{c1::Nebraska Medicine}} (20+ AI use cases in 6 months), {{c2::Trinity Industries}} (90% automation), {{c3::bp}} (nuclear energy), {{c4::American Airlines}} (fleet optimization).
back: Nebraska Medicine, Trinity Industries, bp, American Airlines.

front: {{c1::Ontology SDK 2.0}} was announced at {{c2::DevCon}} in November 2024.
back: Ontology SDK 2.0, DevCon, Nov 2024.

front: Palantir partnered with {{c1::NVIDIA}} to deliver a '{{c2::sovereign AI operating system}}' reference architecture integrating AIP + Foundry + Apollo.
back: NVIDIA partnership for sovereign AI OS.

front: Gotham users include {{c1::CIA}}, {{c2::NSA}}, {{c3::DIA}}, {{c4::FBI}}, {{c5::SOCOM}}, {{c6::DHS}}, and the {{c7::Ukrainian Military}}.
back: CIA, NSA, DIA, FBI, SOCOM, DHS, Ukrainian Military, and more.

front: Explain Palantir's 'enterprise operating system' analogy: how does Foundry map to the concepts of a computer OS?
back: **File System** = Data integration/storage layer. **Kernel** = Ontology (maps data to meaning). **Device Drivers** = Connectors (interface with external systems). **Application Framework** = Workshop, Slate, Object Explorer. **Process Management** = Pipelines, transforms, jobs. **User Permissions** = RBAC, audit trails. **App Store** = Marketplace of pre-built apps. Just as an OS mediates hardware↔applications, Foundry mediates data sources↔operational decisions.

front: Palantir collaborated with {{c1::Lockheed Martin}} in 2022 to modernize naval combat systems using Apollo.
back: Lockheed Martin partnership, 2022, naval combat systems.

front: {{c1::MetaConstellation}} is Palantir's satellite tasking platform that enables autonomous sensor management from space.
back: MetaConstellation — satellite tasking and autonomous sensor management.

front: In computer science, an ontology is a {{c1::formal, explicit specification of a shared conceptualization}} — defining individuals, classes, attributes, relations, and axioms.
back: Formal, explicit specification of a shared conceptualization.

front: The Palantir Ontology is a {{c1::live, editable, governed semantic layer}} that maps raw data to real-world entities, creating a '{{c2::digital twin}}' of the business.
back: Live, editable, governed semantic layer → digital twin.

front: {{c1::Shyam Sankar}} describes the Ontology as a 'digital twin of the organization.'
back: Shyam Sankar — Palantir COO/CTO (context: 'digital twin of the organization').

front: The Ontology's four building blocks are {{c1::Object Types}} (nouns), {{c2::Properties}} (adjectives), {{c3::Links}} (relationships), and {{c4::Actions}} (verbs).
back: Object Types, Properties, Links, Actions.

front: In the Ontology, an Object Type is analogous to a {{c1::table}}, an Object is analogous to a {{c2::row}}, and an Object Set is analogous to a {{c3::result set}} in a database.
back: Object Type=Table, Object=Row, Object Set=Result set.

front: Object Types are created and managed in {{c1::Ontology Manager}}, the administrative interface within Foundry.
back: Ontology Manager — admin interface for Object Types.

front: Palantir recommends {{c1::string-based}} primary keys over {{c2::numeric IDs}} because they support UUIDs, composite keys, and alphanumeric systems.
back: String primary keys preferred (UUID support).

front: The three Link cardinalities in the Ontology are {{c1::One-to-One}}, {{c2::One-to-Many}}, and {{c3::Many-to-Many}}.
A {{c1::self-referencing link}} allows an Object Type to link to itself, e.g., Employee → manages → Employee.
back: Self-referencing link — hierarchical structures within one type.

front: Ontology Links are unlike traditional joins because they are {{c1::named and directional}}, {{c2::traversable at any depth}}, and {{c3::security-aware}} (hidden if user lacks permission).
back: Named/directional, traversable at any depth, security-aware.

front: An Action Type defines a structured operation that {{c1::creates}}, {{c2::modifies}}, or {{c3::deletes}} Objects, Properties, and Links in a governed, auditable way.
back: Creates, modifies, or deletes Objects/Links — governed and auditable.

front: The four elements of an Action are: {{c1::parameter collection}}, {{c2::validation logic}}, {{c3::side effects}}, and {{c4::audit trail}}.
back: Parameter collection, validation, side effects, audit trail.

front: The four types of Actions are {{c1::single-object}}, {{c2::bulk}}, {{c3::form-based}}, and {{c4::function-backed}}.
back: Single-object, bulk, form-based, function-backed.

front: The Ontology's three layers (bottom to top) are: {{c1::Semantic Layer}} ('The Nouns'), {{c2::Kinetic Layer}} ('The Verbs'), and {{c3::Dynamic Layer}} ('The Rules').
back: Semantic (nouns) → Kinetic (verbs) → Dynamic (rules).

front: The {{c1::Semantic Layer}} defines Object Types, Properties, and Link Types — answering '{{c2::What entities exist and how do they relate?}}'
back: Semantic Layer — 'What exists?' — Object Types, Properties, Links. Built by data engineers + DS.

front: The {{c1::Kinetic Layer}} defines Actions, Functions, Writeback, and OSDK — answering '{{c2::How does the organization take action and change state?}}'
back: Kinetic Layer — 'What can happen?' — Actions, Functions, Writeback, OSDK.

front: The {{c1::Dynamic Layer}} defines Business Rules, Policies, Permissions (RBAC), Workflows, and Dynamic Security — answering '{{c2::How does the organization govern and enforce?}}'
back: Dynamic Layer — 'Who can see/do what?' — Rules, RBAC, workflows.

front: Explain how the Palantir Ontology differs from a traditional database schema. Give at least 4 dimensions.
back:
1. **Purpose**: DB schema organizes storage. Ontology models real-world decisions & operations.
2. **Writability**: DB tables are raw CRUD. Ontology enforces governed Actions with validation, side effects, and audit trails.
3. **Semantics**: DB has tables/columns. Ontology has Object Types, Properties, Links — a shared business vocabulary.
4. **Security**: DB has table-level perms. Ontology has object-level, row-level, and column-level RBAC that propagates to every app and AI agent.
5. **AI Integration**: DB can't ground an LLM. Ontology objects serve as the 'truth layer' for AIP to prevent hallucination.
6. **Application layer**: From an Ontology, you can build apps (Workshop) in hours without building an API layer — the Ontology IS the API.

front: {{c1::OSDK}} (Ontology SDK) supports {{c2::TypeScript}}, {{c3::Python}}, and {{c4::Java}} for programmatic access to the Ontology.
back: OSDK — TypeScript, Python, Java.

front: {{c1::Writeback}} is the Ontology capability that pushes changes back to {{c2::source systems}} (e.g., updating SAP after an approval in Foundry).
back: Writeback — push Ontology changes back to source systems.

front: A {{c1::Derived Property}} is computed at {{c2::runtime}} from other properties or links (beta as of late 2024). Example: customer.lifetime_value = SUM of all linked order.total_price.
back: Derived Property — computed at query time, beta feature.

front: A {{c1::Shared Property}} is a property definition reused across multiple Object Types for consistent naming and typing (e.g., created_at_timestamp).
back: Shared Property — reusable property definition across Object Types.

front: {{c1::Backing Dataset}} is the raw data source (Parquet files, tables, API feeds) that feeds an Object Type. The Ontology does not store data independently.
back: Backing Dataset — the raw source; Ontology provides a semantic view, not storage.

front: The five-step Ontology design framework is: {{c1::DISCOVER}} → {{c2::MODEL}} → {{c3::INSTRUMENT}} → {{c4::ACTIVATE}} → {{c5::GOVERN}}.
back: DISCOVER → MODEL → INSTRUMENT → ACTIVATE → GOVERN.

front: DISCOVER asks '{{c1::What decisions do users make?}}'. MODEL asks '{{c2::What entities and relationships support those decisions?}}'.
back: DISCOVER = decisions. MODEL = entities + relationships.

front: INSTRUMENT asks '{{c1::What data sources back these entities?}}'. ACTIVATE asks '{{c2::What actions do users need to perform?}}'. GOVERN asks '{{c3::Who can see and do what?}}'.
back: INSTRUMENT = data sources. ACTIVATE = actions. GOVERN = permissions.

front: Best practice: Model {{c1::business decisions}}, not {{c2::source systems}}. Create Object Types like 'Customer' and 'Order', not 'sap_orders_raw'.
back: Model decisions, not source systems.

front: Best practice: Use {{c1::snake_case}} naming, no {{c2::version suffixes}} (e.g., _v2), and suffix with type hints ({{c3::_timestamp}}, {{c4::_id}}, {{c5::_count}}).
back: snake_case, no version suffixes, add type hints like _timestamp/_id/_count.

front: {{c1::Foundry Branching}} (beta, 2025) allows testing Actions on branches {{c2::without committing to production}}.
back: Foundry Branching — test Actions on branches safely.

front: {{c1::Native Federation}} incorporates external datasets into the Ontology without {{c2::duplicating data}}.
back: Native Federation — query external data without duplication.

front: Explain why Palantir considers the Ontology their 'core IP' and deepest competitive moat.
back:
1. **The Ontology turns raw data into operational decisions** — competitors can copy UIs but can't replicate a deeply integrated, governed, real-time semantic layer over an entire enterprise's data.
2. **Unified interface**: Same objects consumed by Workshop, Slate, AIP agents, OSDK apps, and API gateways — one semantic framework everywhere.
3. **AI grounding**: The Ontology prevents LLM hallucination by constraining AI to verified objects. Without it, enterprise AI is unreliable.
4. **Citizen development**: Because the Ontology IS the API (data model + permissions + actions), non-engineers build apps in hours, not months.
5. **Cross-platform spine**: Same Ontology concept in Gotham, Foundry, and AIP — it's the architectural DNA of everything Palantir builds.

front: The Ontology's '80/20' principle: Without an Ontology, building an app requires 8 steps (requirements → schema → API → frontend → auth → logic → deploy → maintain). With an Ontology, it's just {{c1::2 steps}}: define Objects/Links/Actions → {{c2::drag-and-drop in Workshop}}.
back: 2 steps: define Ontology + build in Workshop. Ontology replaces API, auth, schema, and logic layers.

front: The Semantic Web standards associated with the academic ontology concept include {{c1::W3C}}, {{c2::OWL}}, and {{c3::RDF}}.
back: W3C, OWL, RDF — Semantic Web standards.

front: The {{c1::Developer Console}} is the portal for creating and managing {{c2::OSDK}} applications.
back: Developer Console — manage OSDK apps.

front: A {{c1::Side Effect}} is an additional consequence triggered by an Action, such as sending a notification, updating linked objects, or triggering a pipeline.
back: Side Effect — additional consequence of an Action (notification, pipeline trigger, etc.).

front: Explain the Ontology's three-layer model using the 'dictionary' analogy. What is each layer's analogy and who builds it?
back:
**Semantic Layer** = 'The dictionary and grammar' — defines what nouns exist and how they relate. Built by data engineers + DS.
**Kinetic Layer** = 'The allowed sentences' — the verbs, operations, and workflows that can be performed. Built by engineers + DS.
**Dynamic Layer** = 'The law and governance' — rules determining who can say what, when, and under what conditions. Built by DS, security teams, governance leads.

front: In the Ontology, security policies propagate automatically — if a user can't see a Patient object, they can't see it in {{c1::any application}}, {{c2::any report}}, or {{c3::any AI agent response}}.
back: Security propagates everywhere — apps, reports, AI agents.

front: Palantir's framing: shipping an LLM without an Ontology is like '{{c1::shipping a spreadsheet without a database}}'.
back: 'Shipping a spreadsheet without a database.'
